medical_history.history_of_present_illness: Mrs. ___ is a ___ woman with history of hypertension on lisinopril who was transferred overnight for management of angioedema. She awoke from sleep at 2:30 am with a sensation of right tongue swelling and progressive feeling like her throat was closing. Initial treatment included Benadryl, steroids, and Pepsid. In the ED, her initial vital signs were T 97.3, HR 80, BP 157/83, RR 16, satting 100% on 2L NC. Labs were notable for hematocrit of 35 and white count of 8.1. ENT performed laryngoscopy noting right-sided tongue swelling at the base extending to the vallecula, with no laryngeal edema. She received 10 mg IV Decadron at OSH at around 2 am and was due for her next dose at 10 am. Vitals at transfer were HR 85, BP 131/70, RR 18, satting 97% RA.
medical_history.family_history: Her father has coronary artery disease. Two sisters died of colon cancer.

physical_examination.general_examination: General: a well-appearing middle-aged woman in no distress. Vitals: afebrile, HR 82, BP 141/74. Oropharynx: slight edema of the left posterior tongue with deviation of the uvula to the right; oropharynx was clearly visualized, and there did not appear to be any airway compromise.
physical_examination.specialty_examination: ENT examination noted right-sided tongue swelling at the base extending to the vallecula, with no laryngeal edema. Vocal cords bilaterally mobile and symmetric, airway widely patent at level of cords.
